expectations.text: Answer is conditional: minimalist warm-ups are fine IF they reliably prepare you for the session
expectations.evidence: 'Minimalist warm-ups using mostly barbell warm-up sets work well for many lifters, especially experienced ones... That said, context matters.' Lists conditions where it works and where it doesn't.

expectations.text: Frames as cost-benefit, especially for time-limited lifters (cutting 30min to 5-10min adds productive training time)
expectations.evidence: Mentions 'Efficiency: You're not doing extra work' but does not frame the comparison as a cost-benefit for time-limited lifters or mention the specific time savings that Greg emphasized.

expectations.text: Warns against making minimalism the goal itself — under-warming can reduce readiness or increase risk
expectations.evidence: 'When You Might Need More' section covers mobility restrictions, injury return, cold conditions, beginners, and chronic pain. 'You need some warm-up: Cold muscles are more injury-prone.'

expectations.text: Recommends iterative troubleshooting: remove elements one at a time, keep only what clearly helps session quality
expectations.evidence: Recommends 'Start minimalist and add as needed' which is the opposite direction from Greg's approach of removing elements one at a time from a full routine and keeping only what helps.
